skill.name: fiftyone-pr-triage
skill.description: Triage FiftyOne GitHub issues by validating status, categorizing resolution, and generating standardized responses. Use when reviewing issues to determine if fixed, won't fix, not reproducible, no longer relevant, or still valid.
skill.summary: Guide consistent FiftyOne GitHub issue triage with evidence checks and response templates.
skill.author: AdonaiVera

security_audit.summary: The static external-command findings are partly true because the skill instructs users to run repository search, git, and GitHub CLI commands. The high weak-cryptography and system-reconnaissance findings are false positives from Markdown examples, placeholders, and issue-response text; no malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: External Command Examples Require User Care
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill recommends grep, find, xargs, git, gh, and pip commands for issue investigation. These are legitimate maintainer workflows, but they can read local repository data, call GitHub through gh, or install packages if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in fenced examples and match the skill's triage purpose. They are not hidden or malicious, but they still require execution in a trusted repository context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high-risk weak-cryptography alerts map to ordinary Markdown text, issue categories, placeholders such as commit_hash, and checklist or table content. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or insecure hash use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose, labels, or response-template placeholders rather than cryptographic code. There is no executable crypto operation in the only scanned file.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The scanner flagged issue-category text and response-template environment fields, not commands that enumerate the host system. The OS field is requested as issue context for reproducibility.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms appear in triage prose and environment-report templates. No host enumeration command or data exfiltration path is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Markdown Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes command examples for searching code, inspecting git history, querying GitHub issues and PRs, and upgrading FiftyOne. Users should review substituted search terms and run commands only in intended repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly visible and intentionally part of the workflow. The risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: FiftyOne maintainers need consistent decisions when old or complex GitHub issues pile up. This skill guides evidence gathering, categorization, and standard replies.
actual_capabilities: Classifies issues as already fixed, will not fix, not reproducible, no longer relevant, or still valid.
Guides codebase searches for affected modules, classes, and functions.
Uses git history checks to connect issues with commits, releases, or pull requests.
Searches related closed issues and merged pull requests through GitHub CLI examples.
Provides standard response templates for each triage category.
Defines a checklist for validating evidence before closing or escalating an issue.
limitations: It is specific to the FiftyOne project and its repository structure.
It does not automatically verify issues without access to code, git history, and GitHub data.
It depends on user judgment for reproduction quality and final maintainer decisions.
Some commands require local tools such as git, grep, gh, and pip.

use_cases.title: Clear Stale Issues
use_cases.description: Review old FiftyOne issues and decide whether they are fixed, outdated, duplicated, or still active.

use_cases.title: Prepare Maintainer Replies
use_cases.description: Create consistent comments that explain triage results, evidence, and next steps for issue authors.

use_cases.title: Investigate Bug Reports
use_cases.description: Trace a reported behavior through source files, git history, related issues, and reproduction status.

prompt_templates.title: Classify One Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage of a single issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the FiftyOne issue triage workflow on this issue. Identify the likely category, missing evidence, and the safest next reply.

prompt_templates.title: Check for Existing Fix
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this issue against recent FiftyOne commits and related pull requests. Decide whether it is already fixed and draft a maintainer response.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Reproduction
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the reproduction details, error message, version, and affected module. Tell me whether the report is reproducible or needs more information.

prompt_templates.title: Triage a Batch
prompt_templates.prompt: Triage these FiftyOne issues as a batch. Group them by category, list evidence needed, and provide concise response drafts for each group.

output_examples.input: An issue reports a bug on an old FiftyOne release, and recent commits changed the affected module.
output_examples.output: Category: Already Fixed. The response should point to the relevant release or commit, recommend upgrading, and ask the reporter to confirm.

output_examples.input: A bug report lacks code, traceback, sample data, and environment details.
output_examples.output: Category: Not Reproducible. The response should request a minimal example, complete traceback, version, operating system, and sample data if relevant.

output_examples.input: A feature request conflicts with documented project scope and would break backward compatibility.
output_examples.output: Category: Will Not Fix. The response should explain the design reason, note the compatibility concern, and suggest an alternative when available.

best_practices: Confirm the issue category with direct evidence before drafting a closure comment.
Run command examples only in the intended FiftyOne repository and review substituted search terms.
Keep maintainer replies concise, specific, and linked to commits, pull requests, or reproduction results.
anti_patterns: Do not close issues only because they are old without checking current relevance.
Do not claim a fix exists without identifying the related commit, pull request, or release.
Do not run copied shell commands in unrelated directories or with untrusted substituted input.

faq.question: What project is this skill designed for?
faq.answer: It is designed for triaging GitHub issues in the FiftyOne project.

faq.question: Can it automatically close GitHub issues?
faq.answer: No. It provides investigation steps and response templates. A maintainer must take final action.

faq.question: Does it require command-line tools?
faq.answer: Some workflows reference git, grep, find, xargs, gh, and pip. Review commands before running them.

faq.question: Can it help with feature requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a category for requests that are out of scope, by design, or incompatible with project goals.

faq.question: How does it handle missing reproduction details?
faq.answer: It recommends the not reproducible category and asks for a minimal example, traceback, environment, and sample data.

faq.question: Is it useful outside FiftyOne?
faq.answer: Parts of the triage structure may help other projects, but the commands and templates assume FiftyOne context.
